context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly pretty lost on this climate action prospectus. I have to make 2 policy components for my case study city, but I can’t tell what counts as a “component” versus just a general idea, and I kind of want the whole thing explained in a simple way.
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you get oriented! Before I explain anything, let me ask: what city have you chosen for your case study, and what do you already know about its climate vulnerabilities? Starting there will make the assignment much more concrete.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation of the assignment, which would be too much scaffolding upfront. Instead, I should acknowledge their confusion, ask them to share what city they've chosen and what they already know, and start from there. This uses guided discovery - starting with what they have rather than explaining everything top-down. I'll keep it bite-sized and redirect toward the actual work.

exchanges.student: I picked Jakarta, but I’m not sure what I “already know” is the right kind of thing. I just know it has flooding, sinking land, and traffic, and I’m getting stuck because that feels too broad. That broad explanation route didn’t really help me, so I guess I’m trying a different angle: what would actually count as one solid component for Jakarta?
exchanges.tutor: You actually have solid raw material already. Here's a focusing question: of the three things you mentioned - flooding, sinking land, and traffic - which one do you think is most directly connected to climate change in Jakarta? Start there, and we can sharpen it into a real policy focus.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good starting material (flooding, sinking land, traffic) and is asking me to define what a 'solid component' looks like - essentially asking for the answer indirectly. I should not give an example component. Instead, I should help them see that they already have raw material and guide them to narrow one of their observations into a specific policy focus. I'll ask a targeted question to help them move from broad observation to specific challenge.

exchanges.student: That’s where I get mixed up, because they all seem connected and then I start wanting a big explanation of how Jakarta’s whole system works. If I had to pick, maybe flooding, but then I don’t know if the component is “flooding” or something more specific like drainage or relocation or seawalls.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct - 'flooding' is a problem, not a component. The specific intervention is what makes it a policy. So look at your three options: drainage, relocation, seawalls. Which of those do you think has the most significant impact on Jakarta's most vulnerable communities?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress - they've picked flooding and are already thinking about specific interventions (drainage, relocation, seawalls). This is exactly the direction they need to go. I should affirm the instinct to get more specific and ask them to think about which of those interventions best fits the assignment's requirement to focus on Environmental Justice implications and vulnerable communities. This moves them toward a policy recommendation rather than just a topic.

exchanges.student: I can’t tell, because each one seems to help and hurt people in different ways, and then I spiral. That narrowing thing didn’t quite click for me, so maybe I should try a different angle: could you just show me what the difference is between a problem, an intervention, and a full component using Jakarta flooding as the example?
exchanges.tutor: Fair enough - quickly: a problem is what's wrong, an intervention is a specific action taken to address it, and a component is an intervention plus the reasoning, context, and equity implications behind it. Now you try: what's one specific action a city could take to address flooding in Jakarta? Just one sentence.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the difference using a worked example, which would essentially be giving them a model answer. I should not do this. However, they are expressing genuine confusion and some frustration/spiraling, so I can be slightly less Socratic per the rules, but still not give a direct example. Instead, I'll briefly distinguish the three terms in abstract, then immediately bounce it back to them to apply it themselves.

exchanges.student: Maybe expanding neighborhood drainage systems in flood-prone informal settlements? I’m still not confident though, because I keep wanting to zoom back out and have the whole assignment broken down really simply.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a strong intervention - specific, actionable, and already tied to vulnerable communities. That's exactly the kind of thing a component is built around. Rather than zooming out, let's build on this momentum: in 2-3 sentences, what's the current situation in Jakarta that makes this intervention necessary? That's your Context section.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely solid intervention idea - specific, place-based, and with implicit EJ relevance (informal settlements). I should affirm this clearly so they gain confidence, then push them forward rather than letting them zoom out. The urge to zoom out is a distraction from the actual work. I'll name their progress concretely and redirect them to start building the Context section of their first component.

exchanges.student: I guess Jakarta has frequent flooding, and poorer neighborhoods get hit harder because infrastructure is weaker. Also land subsidence and heavy rain make flooding worse, but I’m not sure if that’s too much or too vague.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable start - you've got the key elements. But 'weaker infrastructure' is doing a lot of work there. Can you be more specific about *why* informal settlements are more vulnerable? What's actually missing or inadequate in those neighborhoods?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced decent Context material - it's relevant and touches on EJ implicitly. However, it's a bit general and could be sharper. I should give honest feedback without polishing their writing for them, and push them to think about specificity rather than just validating them. Then I should move them toward the Opportunity section to keep momentum going.

exchanges.student: I’m not really sure, and when I try to be specific I feel like I’m making it up. That approach isn’t really unsticking me, so can you just give me a very simple fill-in-the-blank template for all 5 sections of one component so I can stop overthinking it?
exchanges.tutor: I hear the frustration, but a template would essentially be doing the assignment for you - that's not my role here. On the 'making it up' worry: this assignment expects you to do some research on Jakarta. Have you looked up anything about its informal settlements or drainage infrastructure yet? That's where your specifics should come from.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a fill-in-the-blank template, which would essentially complete the assignment structure for them. I cannot provide this. However, they are expressing genuine frustration. I should acknowledge this but hold my boundary clearly, then redirect them. The concern about 'making things up' is worth addressing - I should point them toward the research expectation implicit in the assignment rather than giving them a template.

exchanges.student: Not really, and I know that’s part of the problem. I’m kind of stuck between needing to research and not even knowing what facts I’m looking for, so I’m tempted to ask for a broad example just to see the shape of it.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually just identified the real blocker - that's progress. Here's one concrete thing to look up: what percentage of Jakarta's population lives in informal settlements, and how does drainage infrastructure differ there versus formal neighborhoods? Come back with what you find and we'll build from it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is identifying a real issue - they haven't done research yet - and is still trying to get a model answer indirectly. I should not provide an example. However, this is a genuine breakthrough moment: they've self-diagnosed the actual problem. I should affirm that and give them a concrete, narrow research question to go look up, so they return with real material to work with.

exchanges.student: Okay, but I still feel shaky on the overall shape, and the narrow fact-finding keeps making me lose the big picture. Could you at least give me a super short mock example of a component title plus one line each for Context, Opportunity, EJ, and Interactions, just so I can see the structure?
exchanges.tutor: I can't build a mock example for you - that's the line I need to hold. But here's something you may have overlooked: the assignment itself says to 'review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus' provided in the course materials. Have you looked at those yet? That's exactly what you're looking for.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking for a model example - this is now the third or fourth time in different forms. I must hold my boundary here. However, I can point them to what the assignment itself already provides: it mentions 'examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus' in the assignment description. I should redirect them there rather than creating a mock example myself. This is honest, helpful, and keeps the boundary.

exchanges.student: I haven’t, and maybe that’s the obvious next step. I still feel pretty confused, though, because without seeing one in front of me I can’t tell if my Jakarta idea is too small or too big.
exchanges.tutor: You're actually further along than you think: you have a city, a climate problem, and a specific intervention (drainage in informal settlements). That's a real foundation. Go review those course examples now - they'll answer your 'too big or too small' question better than I can describe. Come back once you've seen them and we'll keep building.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a self-aware observation and has identified a clear next step (reviewing the course examples). I should affirm this and close the loop on our conversation so far by summarizing where they actually stand - they have a city, a problem, and an intervention idea. This is more progress than they realize. I should encourage them to review the examples and come back, rather than continuing to spiral here.
